Christ showed through parables that he would set aside the Jewish church. It is sad to think about how poorly God’s faithful ministers have been treated throughout all ages by those who have enjoyed the privileges of the church but have not produced fruit worthy of it. God eventually sent his Son, his Beloved; and it might be expected that those whom their Master loved would also respect and love him; but instead of honoring him because he was the Son and Heir, they hated him. But Christ’s exaltation was the Lord's doing; and it is his doing to exalt him in our hearts and to set up his throne there; and if this is done, it cannot help but be amazing in our eyes. The Scriptures, faithful preachers, and the coming of Christ in human form call on us to render due praise to God in our lives. Let sinners beware of a proud, carnal spirit; if they revile or despise the preachers of Christ, they would have done so to their Master had they lived when he was on earth.

The enemies of Christ might seem eager to know their duty, but really they hoped that whatever side he took on the question, they could find a reason to accuse him. Nothing is more likely to trap followers of Christ than involving them in disputes about worldly politics. Jesus avoided the trap by referring to the submission they had already made as a nation; and everyone who heard him marveled at the wisdom of his answer. Many will praise the words of a sermon, but will not be commanded by its doctrines.

A true knowledge of Scripture, as the source from which all revealed religion now flows and the foundation on which it is built, is the best protection against error. Christ set aside the objection of the Sadducees, who were the mocking skeptics of that day, by clarifying the doctrine of the future state. The relationship between husband and wife, though established in the earthly paradise, will not be known in the heavenly one. It is no wonder if we confuse ourselves with foolish errors when we form our ideas about the spirit world based on the affairs of this physical world. It is absurd to think that the living God could be a person’s portion and happiness if that person is forever dead; therefore, it is certain that Abraham’s soul exists and acts, though now temporarily separated from the body. Those who deny the resurrection are greatly mistaken, and should be told so. Let us seek to pass through this dying world with a joyful hope of eternal happiness and a glorious resurrection.

Those who sincerely desire to be taught their duty, Christ will guide in judgment and teach his way. He tells the scribe that the great commandment, which indeed includes all others, is to love God with all our hearts. Wherever this is the governing principle in the soul, there is a disposition toward every other duty. Loving God with all our heart will lead us to everything that pleases him. The sacrifices only represented atonements for people’s transgressions of the moral law; they had no power except as they expressed repentance and faith in the promised Savior, and led to moral obedience. And because we have not loved God and others, but the opposite, therefore we are condemned sinners; we need repentance, and we need mercy. Christ approved what the scribe said and encouraged him. He was well-positioned for further progress; for this knowledge of the law leads to conviction of sin, repentance, discovery of our need for mercy, and understanding the way of justification by Christ.

When we pay attention to what the Scriptures declare about the person and offices of Christ, we will be led to confess him as our Lord and God; to obey him as our exalted Redeemer. If common people hear these things gladly while learned and distinguished people oppose them, the former are happy, and the latter are to be pitied. And just as sin, disguised with a show of piety, is double iniquity, so its doom will be doubly severe.

Let us not forget that Jesus still sees the treasury. He knows how much, and for what motives, people give to his cause. He looks at the heart and our intentions when giving alms; and whether we do it as unto the Lord, or only to be seen by others. It is so rare to find anyone who would not criticize this widow that we cannot expect to find many who will act like her; yet our Savior commends her, therefore we are sure that she did well and wisely. The feeble efforts of the poor to honor their Savior will be commended in that day when the splendid actions of unbelievers will be exposed to contempt.
